speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was thinking to start with it, maybe you'd tell some general background about how the settlement of this area and the people that came here were like and related to what happened to other places in the Northwest.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Alright, I'll start in on the piece about how this fits into the general settlement of the Northwest. Yes. And that's alright, that's a good way to introduce it because it pinpoints it in history for people who never heard of Moscow, Idaho before. Then I'll come down to the way that these different stories about how Moscow was named.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you want me to do that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I think perhaps mentioning the first people that came through here and what traces they left would be very interesting too. Yeah, fine. So go ahead. Oh yes.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, the history of our community, Sam, parallels the history of the whole Pacific Northwest. We too had the coming of the explorers, followed by the fur seekers, followed by the great soul seekers, followed by the great gold seekers, followed by some form of temporary government, and then lastly followed by the home seekers, the people who would stay and build the churches, the homes, and the schools of the community. We know that in 1805 and 6 Lewis and Clark passed this way, and their diary tells us that they camped along the Clearwater River and that their hunters ranged for about 100 miles. So beyond a doubt they came to Topkenma, which was what this lovely valley was called by the Nez Perce Indians, meaning the place of the little spotted deer. Here they hunted for the deer in the winter and dug canvas here in the summer. These were followed by the fur seekers, and we know that Donald McKenzie built a fur trading post along the north bank of the Clearwater River, and undoubtedly his fur trappers came up the Tinette Panup, which is the South Palouse, and Paradise Creek, looking for the silver fur of the beaver, and we can see the beaver yet digging along those creeks in the early spring. But he left no written record of having passed this way. And we know that the great soul seeker Henry Harmon Spalding rode this way from Lacoy, the place of the white butterfly, to Chimaquane, the place of many springs 25 miles out of Spokane. But he drew no map, and nowhere in his diary does he comment about having ridden this way, but it's in a direct line, and beyond a doubt he passed this way. Following the soul seekers came, of course, the first white men to visit this territory and leave a written record of having passed this way, and that was Isaac Ingalls Stevens, the first governor of Washington Territory. And on May 24, 1855, he met in the lush meadows of Walla Walla to treat with the northwest Indians. They came directly from there through this country, and with him was a great German artist and cartographer, Gustavus Sohon. And he drew a map of the way they came, and they wrote in their diaries about having passed this way. We know that about the west side of Paradise Creek on the 20th of June, they arrived there and camped overnight. And Isaac Ingalls Stevens wrote in his report that you look away to the west, and the ground was just carpeted with a myriad of flowers. They stayed overnight and followed down the slope of Paradise Ridge to cross the Pinatpanup, which is the South Palouse, about at the place where the Chinese gardens now stand. And he wrote then in his diary that along the banks of the creek there were 600 lodges of Nez Perce encamped, and that the canvas was so thick that the women could,

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: in three minutes, walk all winter long. And on the hills above the creek, and this would of course be Indian Hills and University Heights, grazed 2 ,000 head of Nez Perce ponies. And of course among them would be many of the lovely Appaloosa horses, for which Moscow was to become international headquarters. And he also wrote in his diary at this time, here I was astonished at the luxuriousness of the grass and at the richness of the soil. Following the coming of Isaac Ingalls Stevens, we have the coming of the gold seekers. And we know that E .D. Pierce passed this way on the way to Canal Gulch, where gold was discovered in 1860. He was led by Jane Silcott, who was the daughter of old Chief Timothy. The E .D. Pierce party had come by way of Walla Walla, had camped overnight with Timothy. At the Red Wolf campground, here the Indians told them that it wasn't safe to go through the Nez Perce country, that they were resenting the coming of so many white people. So Pierce asked if there was any way around this country. And Timothy's daughter, little Jane, who later became Jane Silcott, 17 at the time, said well she knew a way around and she would lead the company. So coming up Steptoe Canyon, they came in through halfway between Colton and Uniontown and came over as far as the South Palouse. And they followed the South Palouse out through what is now Troy, down to Kendrick, up over the Leland country to Canal Gulch, where gold was discovered. And this, of course, meant wagons west and the cry of gold went out almost overnight. Lewiston, which had just been a meeting place for the Nez Perce Indians, became a tent city of 10 ,000 people. This of course had to lead to some temporary form of government. In 1863, Idaho became a territory. And this, of course, was followed by the coming of the first white settlers to our community. There had been cattle people here, but they had only been temporarily, they'd been gold seekers through. There had been mining people who set up temporary shacks. But in 1870 came Tom Tunney, who was the first white man to settle in Lataw County, down along the Thornton Creek Way, almost on the Washington side. He set up the first homestead. In 1971 came the first covered wagons into the vicinity of Moscow. And in that first group were Asbury Ahman Llewellyn and his family, the Haskins family. Mrs. Llewellyn was a Haskins. Came the George Washington Tomers. Came Bill Frazier and his family. Murdo Cameron and his brother Daniel came that same year. The William Taylors, who was considered one of the founding fathers of Moscow, settled out along the South Palouse, where the Lataw County nursing home now stands. He soon established a large garden and planted out trees. And later wagon trains spoke often of having stayed with the William Taylor people while they looked for other homesteads to settle in this community. There are many stories about how Moscow got its name and one thing about them, about folklore. There's many ways of telling it as there are people to do the telling. But we do know that the Indians had called it Tatkanma, meaning the place of the young deer. The first settlers noticed what good food the camas roots were for the Indian people. They were nourishing and they grew fat on it. They thought it might be a good place to raise hogs. So many of the men brought in hogs in the early days and because the hogs too prospered and grew fat on the camas bulbs, they started calling the place Hog Haven. Well, with the coming of the settlers' wives and the wagon trains into the vicinity, they thought Hog Haven was a poor redress. You couldn't very well write home and tell them you were living in Hog Heaven. So the women suggested that the name be changed at least to Paradise and Paradise Hill and Paradise Creek, Paradise Villa, Still Beer, that name. And the name, there were many other stories about how it was named and one is that Asbury Ahman Llewellyn, who was considered one of Moscow's first settlers, had a cow that got out at night and ate up one of the neighbor's flower gardens. And the lady was very upset about it the next morning. But Mr. Llewellyn said, well now you can't blame it on me because it was Ma's cow that did that. So the name became Moscow. And the other story is that some of the founding fathers were sitting in a barn trying to think of what they would call the new settlement. And the cow stuck her head through the window. Somebody said, well there's a cow with moss on her back, so we'll name the place Moscow. But those I think were just folklore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this story that said that there were Russian settlers?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you say that too?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, we can say that. Some people thought there might be or hadn't been Russian settlers. But I helped Dr. Brosnan, who was an authority on Idaho history, research it one time. And we could find no Russian names on any of the tax records or in any of the early day voting records in the county. So we finally wrote to the son of the man who had the name changed. And his name was Mr. Neff. And his father had been postmaster when the name was changed from Paradise Valley to Moscow. And he said that his father, Mr. Neff, had come from a little town called Moscow in Paradise Valley, in western Pennsylvania. And he had moved to a place called Moscow in Iowa. And he thought that his father had called it Moscow, Idaho, remembering the two towns where he had once lived. And that seems a logical solution to me because Moscow was very much in the news in the early part, in the early years of the 1800s. It was a time of Napoleon's historic retreat through the snow and the blizzards of the Russian country. And there were about 16 towns all together in the United States named Moscow. And most of them were named for Moscow, Russia in the early part of the 1800s.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Father Cataldo writes that in some of his papers that he and Henry Harmon Spaulding camped at a spring north of where Moscow is. But Henry Harmon Spaulding never mentions it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that he ever did any more than just ride through here on his way to someplace else?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I think not. I don't think there was any real cause, although this was, this would have been a good resting place like overnight. But I don't suppose there were enough people here to stop and talk religion to. There was nobody here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they establish the place where they put the cross up on Highway 95 as being the place that they stopped? Do you know how they ever figured that out?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, there's a spring out there in what they call Section 9.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And I think that that was how they did it. There's a spring there, and in Father Cataldo's papers, he wrote that they stopped at a spring and stayed overnight and would visit. So I think they thought that the spring there on Section 9 would be in about a direct route from Spaulding to Spokane. But the cross on the highway, it was put out on the highway because there's no use in marking a spring that's way off the highway where no one can see it. The cross and the marker were put on the highway where people could know that this was a general location of the place where they had met.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me about the Indian Trail itself that came through here?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, there were two great Indian trails that ran this way that had been used from prehistoric times. And of course, the one came up from Coyote Grade, came up through the Genesee Country, came up on the east side of Paradise Butte, which would be what we call through the Blaine Country, and passed through that way, came down about where the Red Wolf marker is. And that was the Greater Nez Perce Trail. Now the Red Wolf Trail came up from the Red Wolf Campground, which was at Alpawa. And that was Old Timothy's home, and it was Spaulding's first Christian convert. And he called his place Alpawa because it was derived from words that sounded that way, that meant the place of Sabbath rest. Old Timothy had early adopted the ways of the missionaries. He wouldn't let anyone work on Sunday. So he called his place Alpawa, meaning the place of Sabbath rest. And the Red Wolf Trail ran from there up through what is now a step called Steptoe Canyon. And it came up through Thorn Creek. It crossed the divide and then skirted the west edge of Paradise Ridge. And there was a spring there at the west edge, and it was still called a Stevens Spring, because here is where the map shows that Isaac Ingalls Stevens with his young 13 -year -old son Hazard, and his crew of 22 mountain men and packers camped overnight. And the trail then followed on down and crossed the Tenet Pennup, and went on out to the east, where it joined the Greater Nez Perce Trail. And there is a marker over in that general vicinity, maybe a mile from where the actual meeting place of the two trails was. But it marked, anyway, the school children put it there to mark the meeting place of the Nez Perce Trail with the Red Wolf Trail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Roughly where would that be?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, there was a little school over there called the Mount Thomas School. And there's a marker there put there by the Worthwhile Club. And they put it there because the school children could see it and appreciate their history, rather than over farther along the South Palouse Creek, which would have been about a mile to the north. And beyond that, the Indians ruled that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the first settlers that came into this country use those trails?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh yes, I think so. The Indian trails across the whole United States were the forerunners of the great national highways. They were the forerunners of the railroads that crossed the United States. Of course, the Indians had followed the game trails, the early game trails of the Northwest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that Nez Perce Trail follow roughly the course that 95 takes, going north and south?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so. I think you could say that.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Although they would take the shortcut right up over the Lewiston Bluffs instead of riding down the river. They just came straight up the hill and on across.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about evidence of Lewis and Clark actually having been in this county? How close do you think they really came? Do you think they actually were up on the Potlatch Creek and in that area?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, nobody knows. They drew no map and left no written record of having been here. But we just think if they were out hunting that it's quite likely that their hunters and trappers range this far. But they left no written record to prove that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what about Indian use of this country? What kind of use was there and how much do you think there was?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, we know that we have the written record of the of Isaac Ingalls Stevens that there were about 600 lodges camped here. He's the first white man that left any record of having been here. So, and then it was in June and of course the camels would just be going out of bloom and you could easily find the bulbs and they were digging the bulbs. And beyond a doubt they had come here for many many years and oh and the anthropologists and archaeologists have passed this way and they've studied it say that beyond a doubt they use this country as a hunting ground and as a canvas digging ground and they have left their names on the land. Our, the name of our county is Laytaw and that comes from two Nez Perce words meaning the place of pestle and pine. So we know there must have been many pestle rocks here that they gathered to do their grinding with. Of course we've all seen the evidence and the evidence still stands of the pine trees so I think that was a very fitting name for it.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Potlatch of course meant the place for the exchange of gifts and the very earliest settlers tell about how the Indians would come this far on the way to meet with the Coeur d 'Alene's and this was a nice grassy place where many cattle could be could be fed in the summer and the horses could feed and when the Indians went to any of their big meetings they'd take their horses along to fatten them up if it was on a fur trading mission. They altered way many many more horses than they could possibly need to pack or to ride but they took them along so they could graze and get fat in the summertime on all the rich bunch grass and this was a favorite place to meet and race their horses and meet with the Coeur d 'Alene Indians and the Spokane Indians and there have been evidences that the archaeologists could cite to you of them having been here.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I guess I have heard on the north end of the county that Coeur d 'Alene Indians were around Potlatch Princeton area so oh yes so I suppose that that may mean that this was a place where they more or less came together.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes I think that I think we could safely say that because the very first settlers in the community tell about meeting the young the young Indians and riding horses. George, young George Tomer who was George Worshington Tomer's son often told about how he raced his horses with the Indian boys along the Tenet Panup and what good times they had together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In 1877 when the Nez Perce war broke out, what have you heard about the fort that was constructed here and about what the people did about that war?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes I knew some of the people who were in the fort of course I wasn't born then but I have heard Lily Lou Allen who was one of the very first white children born in this vicinity. I heard many of them tell about how they built. There was more than one fort built. There was a fort built up toward the Ursuline Academy and the DAR had marked that many years ago while some of the original people were still alive and I can remember seeing the logs of that fort scattered around in the empty lots. Many when I was a very young girl going to school and they thought that the Chief Joseph uprising they might come this way to meet with the Coeur d 'Alene's but through the efforts of Father Cataldo and Father Josette and some of the other Catholic missions from the north they persuaded the Coeur d 'Alene's to not go on the war path and they didn't rise to help Chief Joseph from the Nez Perce's. But there was and there was another fort built in the south edge of Moscow down on what would be the Jimmy Deacon land down about where the where the where the oil companies are along the Sweet Avenue. There was a fort to put up there too and right out here in our own community there's a high hill down back of the Ed Snow place and I heard him tell that they put up he'd come in 77 and the the log stockade was still on top of that hill because the people thought it was a high hill and if they put up a stockade there they could see if the Indians were coming up from the south they'd see the many miles off and could all get into the stockade. So those are three of the early forts that I've heard talked about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How seriously did they did they take the situation did they stay in these stockades day after day?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well I think maybe the one in Moscow the Howard Fort probably they did but the one down here on the snow land that I don't think they really did any staying and that they just put it up as a safeguard in case they needed it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you did you hear any any stories from the old -timers about what it was like and how how they felt? I would imagine they would be pretty young at the time but during this period of time when they were in stockade?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes they were yes they were very frightened and and the men made quite a thing of it some of them were men home from the civil war so they sort of elected captains and so on and they stood guard and they had rifles and that sort of thing and I think some of them were kind of playing soldier you know.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I was curious about how how the people would manage to to live they'd have to have food and water and a place to sleep and all that in the stockade I would imagine it would be a very difficult situation for people.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well I think I think it probably was I think it probably I think it was they some of them had to go out and get food and bring it into them and so on but Do you imagine that this meant that the that the whole town of Moscow was actually living in the stockade during that time?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well there were very many people in Moscow at that time yes I think there were quite a few people there. I think maybe all about all the people like would be in the immediate vicinity of what was Moscow probably went to the stockade.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder how long that whole scare lasted when the people finally decided it was all right would it have been after after Joseph was out of the country entirely do you think?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well I just don't remember what yes yes and that was the last of October that he surrendered at the Battle of the Verepar and I imagine I should think they got out of there before we came on myself.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I have you heard any I've read someplace I think maybe it was in the bunch grass historian or someplace else I read something about the

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Coeur d 'Alene's trying to trying to assure the local people that they were not going to be in the war.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember anything about that?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes I think there was and they I think there was something to that effect that they weren't going they were going to try and help protect the white people. The early Jesuit missionaries that had a very strong influence over the Coeur d 'Alene's and I think they were very fundamental in helping hold them from coming down and joining up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So in fact the early settlers here weren't in danger even though they believed that they were perhaps?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No I don't think they were really in any danger at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were measures that were taken here taken many other places in Idaho and at the same time do you know?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well I think maybe out around Idaho City and in that country maybe there were I just couldn't answer that.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: What about the influence of Spaulding on the Nez Perce was do you feel that

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: his influence was very strong on the people and so far as the way that they felt about white people? Was there a division in the tribe?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh yes, oh yes I think there's always been quite a division in the tribe between the Christians and the heathen India and what they called the heathens the ones that weren't the followers of the of Spaulding I think it was always quite a division.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that was Joseph was the heathen part of the Nez Perce?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well old Chief Joseph had been Henry Harmon's one of Henry Harmon Spaulding's very first converts Timothy and Joseph and old Chief Joseph had been very devout but he loved the beautiful Valawas and when he dying he had laid the charge at young Chief Joseph's door he said I want to be buried at Lake Wallowa and his bones rest there today but he said you must never sell the bones of your father and mother you must fight for the beautiful Valawas and all the tribes knew that young Chief Joseph who was really a peace -loving man that he would dig up the war hatchet and take to the warpath in defense of the beautiful Valawas and while the treaty of 1855 gave the beautiful Valawas to old Chief Joseph and all his descendants the treaty of 1863 took it away from them again and forced him and all his followers onto the reservation and old Chief Joseph came out of the Valawas and met with the white people Miles and Howard at Lapway for the signing of the treaty of 1863 and while he was seated at the table among all the brass and the braids of the United States Army he came in with his braids and his blanket but under the blanket he carried the white man's covenant the white man's promise of fair dealing and of the brotherhood of men and as he sat there listening he realized what was happening that they were taking the Valawas away from him and going to force that Henry Harmon's falling had given to him he had this he had the book of Matthew under his blanket and this was the white man's covenant the promise of equality in the brotherhood of man and as he sat there listening and he thought if we're going to put him on the reservation and take his beautiful Valawas from him out from under the blanket he drew the covenant and he ripped the pages out of it and tore it into thousands of pieces he threw them in the faces of Howard and Miles the people assembled there at the table and he walked out to symbolize that was just nothing but a piece of paper just scraps of paper that you could tear up and throw away and Henry Harmon Spalding wrote in his diary old chief Joseph has gone back to Egypt he probably preferred the savages to the kind of Christianity they were exhibiting there at the there at the treaty table so he uh he denounced the treaty and was not a part of it no no no no he had nothing to do with it and he walked out on them and let them know it by throwing the torn up scraps of paper from the bible in the faces of people sitting there so that was one of the great dramatic moments of history all right well then then what was it that happened in in 1877 that brought the uh the war about well I suppose it was the the forcing them onto the reservation they were going to force all of the chief Joseph band from the Valawas to come onto the reservation old chief Joseph was dead by that time and young chief Joseph was leading them together with his young young brother olicut

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: and when when chief Joseph said the young men are dead he the young he who led the young man is dead he was referring to his young brave young brother young chief olicut he was he who led the the young people instead is it true from what you know that Joseph was not the war leader of the people but that the decisions were made by uh olicut and several of the other uh Nez Perce yes I think so we've always we've always maintained that that chief Joseph in the great march that he'd been a great general and he'd out maneuvered the fight people through all those miles but it wasn't because he wanted to particularly fight but it was a way to to save the women and the children the old and the feeble the wounded he had all of them in his party and he hoped to escape up into Canada and rendezvous with sitting bull who had just defeated the Custer at the battle of the of the little bighorn and they were rendezvous in Canada and Joseph almost made it the the battle of the bear fall was within less than 50 miles of the Canadian border and some of them did escape across into Canada and to sitting bulls camp and didn't come back for many years but Joseph surrendered there and it was the the winter and not the white men who defeated him

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Q.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it true that they that the Nez Perce were forced to cross the Snake River in the spring floods?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: A.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh yes that yes uh -huh they ordered them down from the Wallowa country to bring the cattle and the horses and oh they had they had asked not to have to come till the water went down because it was too hard to ford the river but they insisted on them coming many of the cattle and and stock were drowned in the crossing there and there is a marker down along the river that commemorates that crossing.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Q.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know whether the the people on the reservation the Nez Perce reservation whether their attitude whether they remember the war very much in later years and whether they they got a more favorable attitude towards Joseph than they had at the time as I understood it the feelings at the time were not so good towards the rebel Nez Perce the one that didn't want to be on the reservation?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: A.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well yes I think that over the years we've come to see the great injustice of it the great inhumanity of it I think that people have long felt that a great wrong was done to these people and I think it took a lot of civilization to bring this feeling about but I do think that in the last in the last 50 years we've seen great strides made to try to redress the wrong that was done to these people many different acts of congress have helped we we made we let them be American citizens with full voting privileges and so on we gave them permission to sue the government for redress they brought have brought suit in many cases and have won reparations for the wrong that's been done them but that's only in the last in the last 50 years at the end of the first world war we found that some of our best fighters had been our own Indian people they knew what it was to fight in defense of our homeland and they were ready to go to war and took many of them took very heroic parts for the defense of the United States in all the wars and we got a new feeling toward them that a great wrong had been done them and that we've wanted to make reparation and and they have received greater recognition for education to train them to do other kinds of things and I think the Upward Bound Program is one of the really wonderful things because it spots potential students who could go on to college with a little encouragement and a little help and get degrees and go back and make finer contributions to their people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well in the early days when the when the settlement was first made around here would you say from what you know that the whites and Indians got along rather well or was there was there much where places where there was much friction between them?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well I can't remember of course in this country hearing much about it because they didn't do much real settling around in here their homes were down as far as the Nez first went their homes were down mostly along the Clearwater River where it was warmer and milder and and this was more hunting country where they rode through and passed through it wasn't like their homes where they tried to live and

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well I guess what I'd like to start talking about settle about the settlement the early years in the settlers and when you mentioned these people that that were among the first people that that came in could you talk more about them and what and what sorts of problems they had to deal with when they were here and there was nothing else and I mean you know those those very first 10 -15 years in this in this country.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well all I can can say is things have been told to me about the early settlers it was my privilege of course to know very well Lily Llewellyn who was the daughter of Asbury Amun Llewellyn and she left many many interesting things that she remembered as a little child and we always considered her the first white child born in the Moscow vicinity and one of the first schools had established at the Haskins place which was east of Moscow and her father's her father's brother was Noah Llewellyn and he was a trained Baptist minister and he preached in the Baptist church here in Moscow and established one of the first schools one of the first schools was called the Haskins school and it was first held out at the Haskins homestead which was east of where Moscow is now and Asbury and Noah Llewellyn taught there and later when they moved to Moscow when the Llewellyn family moved to Moscow then Noah Llewellyn taught at the first Moscow school and well it was the chief trading place of course was cleared Walla Walla and Asbury Amun Llewellyn ran the first store for Moscow first they had a little store out at Paradise Valley which is east of the present town of Moscow about where the across from where the McDonald where the junior high school now stands and there are four big box big poplar trees that mark the plates for that first post office stayed and a small store there then Asbury Amun Llewellyn moved and traded the store to Mr Neff who became the postmaster and he moved to into what is now Moscow and that was in the from 6th street and main street it would be the north west quarter of Moscow was the Llewellyn addition to Moscow and Asbury Amun Llewellyn street and Lily street are all named for that family was this a trade with Neff or did he trade Neff for that land yes they traded and Neff had been became postmaster out there where Asbury Amun Llewellyn had been first and here they had a little store and Mr Llewellyn had to go to Walla Walla to stock his store for the first years that he rented and I've heard Lily tell about how they kept the mail in the shoebox and did you so few letters come you could throw them all in a little shoebox and keep the mail for a week until the people wrote in to see if they had any any any mail so the mail wasn't very heavy into Moscow in those days and as time as time went on

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
